structured.intro: The Strait of Hormuz has functionally closed to commercial shipping. IRGC forces seized **MSC Francesca and Epaminondas** on **22 April**, bringing confirmed vessel incidents to **six in sixteen days**. Transit volumes have collapsed to **7 vessels/day** against a **110/day baseline** — a **94% reduction**. War-risk premium is **unquotable** across Lloyd's syndicates following the **Skylight constructive total loss** on 10 April, and AG–East VLCC rates have reached **$165k/day**, roughly **six times the pre-crisis anchor**.
structured.directionText: Risk trajectory continues to deteriorate across physical, political, and market dimensions. No credible stabilising catalyst is visible in the coming two weeks.
structured.primaryDriver: IRGC operational tempo is the dominant factor. Five physical incidents involving named vessels in sixteen days, including one confirmed constructive total loss, places the strait outside standard underwriting parameters.
structured.contrarian: ADNOC sovereign tankers continue to transit under escort, indicating selective rather than total closure — a narrow signal that commercial traffic could resume quickly if Iranian posture shifts.
structured.changeCondition: A multi-week absence of new physical incidents, combined with Lloyd's syndicates re-opening quotes at any price, would be the minimum condition for de-escalation recognition.

inputs.carriersLimited: COSCO (limited, under escort); ADNOC (active, sovereign status)
inputs.incidents: MSC Francesca and Epaminondas seized by IRGC Navy Apr 22, directed to Iranian coast; Euphoria targeted by IRGC Apr 22, grounded off Iranian coast; Elpis (sanctioned Iranian shadow fleet tanker) transited in defiance of US naval blockade Apr 14; Skylight (VLCC) missile strike Apr 10, constructive total loss; MKD Vyom (LR2) drone strike Apr 8, diverted to Fujairah
